ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for canned fruit salad in syrup; A00LC (processed fruit products) used as closest parent category. Exact code should be verified against EFSA FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical entity for fruit salad; not clinically coded as a distinct food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of canned fruit salad is approximately 150 g (about one cup or a small serving bowl), providing roughly 77 kcal and 16.5 g of carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit Salad — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04020002 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for canned mixed fruit salad in syrup found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: canned fruit cocktail in syrup (GI ~55, medium), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (16.5 g per 150 g serving) / 100 = 9.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g); high natural fruit sugar (11 g/100 g) may drive insulin-mediated sodium retention at large portions
hypertension.guidance: Canned fruit salad in syrup is acceptable for hypertension management given its very low sodium content (4 mg/100 g), but portion size should be limited to 100–150 g per serving to avoid excess sugar intake that may indirectly elevate blood pressure through insulin-mediated sodium retention; avoid adding sweetened syrups or dressings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (11 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) and medium GI (~55); unfavourable carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio accelerates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit canned fruit salad in syrup to a maximum of 100–150 g per serving due to its high sugar content (11 g/100 g), low fibre (1.1 g/100 g), and medium GI (~55), which together produce a glycemic load of approximately 9 per serving and may cause clinically significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; fresh lower-GI fruits such as berries, apple, or pear are preferred alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero saturated fat, very low sodium (4 mg/100 g); moderate sugar content warrants portion control
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned fruit salad in syrup poses minimal cardiovascular risk due to its negligible fat (0.1 g/100 g), confirmed zero saturated fat, and very low sodium (4 mg/100 g); however, the high sugar content (11 g/100 g) should be moderated to avoid contributing to hypertriglyceridaemia, and portion size should be kept to 100–150 g per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant-rich mixed fruits provide vitamin C and polyphenols that reduce oxidative stress; negligible fat and sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Canned fruit salad in syrup is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the mixed fruits provide antioxidants and vitamin C that may reduce inflammatory markers; avoid adding sweetened dressings or syrups, and monitor for individual citrus sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fruit Salad (Canned, in Syrup) is a processed fruit dish consisting of mixed canned fruits — typically peaches, pears, grapes, cherries, and pineapple — preserved in a sugar-based syrup. It is a widely consumed convenience food available in supermarkets globally and commonly served as a dessert, snack, or light accompaniment in Western and international cuisine contexts. The texture is soft and tender due to the canning process, and the flavour is sweet with mild fruity notes, enhanced by the sugar syrup. Per 100 g, it provides 51 kcal, 11 g of carbohydrates (all as sugar), 0.4 g of protein, 0.1 g of fat, 1.1 g of dietary fibre, and 4 mg of sodium. The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (approximately 10:1) makes it nutritionally less favourable than fresh fruit for blood glucose management. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), yielding a glycemic load of about 9 per 150 g serving. Clinically, it is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without strict portion control, and should be consumed cautiously by those managing cardiovascular risk due to its sugar content. It is suitable for hypertension management given its very low sodium. The food is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible across dietary restrictions. It is sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition database with high confidence.
common_user_queries: How many calories in canned fruit salad?
Is canned fruit salad good for weight loss?
What is the sugar content of fruit salad?
Is fruit salad in syrup high GI?
Can I eat fruit salad if I have high blood pressure?
What fruits are in canned fruit salad?
How much fruit salad can a diabetic eat?

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F04020002, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). The input record is canonical and provenance is documented. GI is an analogous estimate (55, medium) from the closest analogous approved-source entry (canned fruit cocktail in syrup, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0: confirmed zero — canned mixed fruit in syrup contains no animal fat or tropical oil; this is consistent with the HPB source reporting fat at 0.1 g/100 g total, with saturated fat confirmed negligible/zero for this food type. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0: naturally zero — this is a plant-based fruit product containing no animal-derived ingredients. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item (F04020002) as of April 2026. USDA FoodData Central entry for fruit salad (canned, in juice/syrup) provides partial micronutrient data but was not used as primary source to maintain HPB provenance consistency; cross-reference with USDA FDC ID 168153 (Fruit salad, canned, water pack) is recommended for future enrichment. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on canned fruit cocktail in syrup from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Direct GI testing of this specific HPB product has not been identified in the literature. 4. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest parent category for processed fruit products; exact code should be verified against the EFSA FoodEx2 browser. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311737 (fruit salad food product) is used as the closest defensible match. 5. The input description states 'canned mixed fruits combined in a sugar-based syrup'; this is consistent with the food classification as a processed fruit dish. The health_suitability entries from the input have been reviewed and incorporated into health_context with updated condition keys and clinical specificity. The hypertension entry in the input contained an internal inconsistency (stating 'suitable: false' while the reasoning supported low concern); this has been resolved by setting concern_level to 'low' with appropriate clinical guidance. 6. This food is Western/International in cuisine origin; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dishes do not apply. local_names fields are null accordingly.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for GI 55, as per standard GI classification (GI 55 is the upper limit for low GI foods). Updated natural_language_description accordingly.
